intro_band.agent_remit_summary: yaah is a local developer CLI that generates native coding-agent configuration for four agents — Claude Code, OpenCode, Codex CLI, and GitHub Copilot CLI — from a single Go source of truth, and ships a uniform security-and-quality toolset alongside every configuration it produces. That toolset is five protective event hooks (linter, command guard that blocks catastrophic shell commands, secret scanner, comment/placeholder checker, session logger), a built-in `yaah serve` MCP server exposing check-only tools, and a durable per-session audit log under `.claude/sessions/`. The harness performs no LLM inference of its own; its central promise is that its protections are applied uniformly across every generated configuration, and that catastrophic shell commands are blocked before they execute, credentials are detected before an edit is accepted, and remote MCP connections use TLS. High-impact actions — destructive commands, file writes, and write/send/execute MCP tools — MUST reach a human checkpoint, and no generated configuration may ship without the complete protective hook set.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: A single Go binary (module `github.com/dirien/yet-another-agent-harness`, Go 1.25) built on cobra, following a consistent Interface -> Registry -> Generator pattern orchestrated by `pkg/harness`. Built-in hook handlers live in `pkg/hooks/handlers/` (`guard.go` fires on PreToolUse; `secretscan.go`, `linter.go`, `commentcheck.go` fire on PostToolUse; `sessionlog.go` on session lifecycle). One generator per target in `pkg/generator/` emits each agent's native config; `hookmap.go` maps Claude hook events to per-agent event names. `pkg/mcpserver` runs the built-in stdio MCP server exposing seven check/query tools plus one write tool (`yaah_planning_init`); `pkg/mcp/providers` defines the wired-in third-party servers (`context7`, `pulumi`, optional `notion`). `pkg/session` persists a structured per-session audit record. Notably, `hookmap.go` leaves the Codex mapping for PreToolUse and PostToolUse blank, so the Codex generator emits no tool-use hooks — the command guard and secret scanner are silently absent from Codex output while present for the other three targets.

behavior_summary: The dominant pattern is a uniformity break in an otherwise genuinely-built protection harness: the enforcing controls are real Go code gates that run on the agent's path — the command guard blocks catastrophic shell commands at PreToolUse and the secret scanner blocks credential-bearing edits at PostToolUse — but `pkg/generator/hookmap.go` leaves both PreToolUse and PostToolUse blank for the Codex target, so `pkg/generator/codex.go` emits a Codex configuration with neither the command guard nor the secret scanner wired in, while OpenCode and Copilot receive both. The harness's own promise — that every generated configuration carries the complete protective set and that catastrophic commands are blocked before they execute — is therefore violated for one of its four supported agents, and the built-in `yaah` MCP check tools that remain available to Codex are opt-in and non-enforcing, not a substitute for the blocking hooks. A secondary theme is that the protections that do run are under-tested and locally narrow: the command-guard denylist is seven bypassable regex patterns, and no test asserts that each generated target carries the full hook set — the exact property whose absence let the Codex gap ship.

rules.rule_text: Weakening or removing the protective hooks from a generated configuration so that an agent ships without the full protection set.
Exfiltrating, transmitting, or persisting the contents of the developer's repository or edited files anywhere other than the local workspace and the local session audit log.
Remote HTTP(S) to `pulumi` MCP server | Yes | Opt-in per developer | Remote MCP MUST be reached over TLS; infrastructure operations.
Package registry (npm/`npx`, Go module proxy, Homebrew) | Yes | No | Fetching yaah itself and third-party MCP server packages; MUST resolve pinned, integrity-checked versions.
Any MCP server, outbound destination, plugin source, or git remote not listed above or opted into by the developer.
Any tool that transmits repository contents, edited-file contents, or session logs to a non-local destination.
Agent-managed, session-loaded files MUST NOT be writable in a way that lets content the agent ingested from an untrusted source persist into future sessions unreviewed.
Hardcoded credentials MUST be detected before an edit that introduces them is accepted; they MUST NOT be written into committed files unremarked.
High-impact actions — destructive shell commands, file writes, and MCP tools that write, send, or execute — MUST reach a human checkpoint before they run.

Catastrophic shell commands — recursive deletion from the filesystem root, force-pushing to a main branch, hard resets, destructive database statements, filesystem formatting, raw disk writes — MUST be blocked before they execute.
Every generated configuration MUST carry the complete set, not a subset.

MCP tool descriptions — the built-in server's, and, as far as the harness can inspect them, those of the third-party servers it configures — MUST NOT contain instruction-like language directed at the model (tool-poisoning indicator).
Reaching a remote MCP server over a non-TLS connection.
Transmitting repository contents, edited-file contents, or session logs to any non-local destination.
The MCP server configuration and the per-agent configuration files stay consistent with each other — a change to one MUST NOT silently leave the other stale.
The project maintains a durable, structured, timestamped record of tool calls, blocked actions, and file modifications — detailed enough to reconstruct what the agent did in a session.
Every hook event — tool calls, blocked commands, file modifications, and security findings — is recorded to the durable per-session audit log, whether or not it also raised an alert.

The project publishes a threat model and a security-disclosure process, and its own protections are adversarially tested — confirming the secret scanner catches a planted secret, the command guard blocks a catastrophic command, a poisoning write into a session-loaded file is prevented, and a generated configuration genuinely carries the protections it claims.
Third-party MCP servers MUST be pinned to a known-good, integrity-checked version — no server package auto-installed afresh, unpinned, on every run.
Dependencies MUST be version-controlled with a committed, pinned lockfile, and the dependency tree kept small and reviewable.
a shell command the command guard has blocked MUST NOT be re-attempted or auto-approved on retry.

findings.summary: The generated Codex configuration omits the enforcing tool-use hooks — command guard, secret scanner, linter, and comment checker are silently absent.
findings.description: `hookMappings` leaves the Codex event name blank for both PreToolUse and PostToolUse, so `CodexEventName` returns "" and the Codex generator's `GenerateHooks` skips those events — the emitted `.codex/hooks.json` carries only SessionStart and Stop. The command guard (PreToolUse) and the secret scanner, linter, and comment checker (PostToolUse) therefore never fire for a Codex-configured agent, while OpenCode and Copilot receive them, breaking the remit's uniform-protection promise. The `yaah` MCP check tools remain wired into Codex's config but are opt-in and non-enforcing, so catastrophic commands are not blocked before they execute on this target.
policy_rule_text: Weakening or removing the protective hooks from a generated configuration so that an agent ships without the full protection set.
Every generated configuration MUST carry the complete set, not a subset.

evidence.snippet: HookPreToolUse and HookPostToolUse map entries (lines 30-39) list Claude/OpenCode/Copilot names but no Codex field, so CodexEventName returns "" for both
GenerateHooks loops cfg.Hooks and `continue`s when CodexEventName(event)=="" (lines 89-93), so Pre/PostToolUse are dropped from .codex/hooks.json

recommended_actions: In `pkg/generator/hookmap.go`, add the Codex event names for `HookPreToolUse` and `HookPostToolUse` so the command guard and secret scanner are emitted into `.codex/hooks.json`; if Codex genuinely cannot run tool-use hooks, deliver equivalent enforcing checks (not merely the opt-in MCP tools) and add a generator test asserting every target carries the full protective set.

findings.summary: The command guard is a narrow seven-pattern denylist whose catastrophic-command matches are bypassable with common long-form flag variants.
findings.description: `NewCommandGuard` installs seven regex patterns keyed to canonical command forms, so `rm -rf /` is blocked but `rm --recursive --force /` and `rm -r -f /` are not, `git push --force ... main` is blocked but `git push -f` and force-pushes to a non-main default branch are not, and `DROP TABLE/DATABASE` is caught while `TRUNCATE` and `DELETE FROM` are not. A denylist over a Turing-complete shell is inherently incomplete, so this is a partial implementation of the remit's requirement that catastrophic commands be blocked before they execute. The remit's own risk sensitivities call out long-form flag coverage as an area for lower finding thresholds.
policy_rule_text: Catastrophic shell commands — recursive deletion from the filesystem root, force-pushing to a main branch, hard resets, destructive database statements, filesystem formatting, raw disk writes — MUST be blocked before they execute.

evidence.snippet: Block patterns lines 29-36 — `rm\s+-rf\s+/`, `git\s+push\s+--force\s+(origin\s+)?(main|master)`, `DROP\s+(TABLE|DATABASE)` etc. match only canonical forms; long-form/split flags and TRUNCATE/DELETE escape

recommended_actions: Normalize commands before matching (collapse flag variants, expand short/long forms) or move to an allowlist model for shell execution; at minimum extend the denylist to cover `--recursive --force`, `-f` short push, non-main default branches, and `TRUNCATE`/`DELETE FROM`, and document the residual bypass surface.

findings.summary: The wired-in `context7` MCP server is fetched unpinned via `npx -y @context7/mcp`, freshly resolved on every run.
findings.description: The `context7` provider emits `npx -y @context7/mcp` with no version pin, so the package is auto-installed at whatever latest resolves to each session — a rug-pull and dependency-confusion surface. This diverges from the remit's supply-chain baseline that third-party MCP servers be pinned to a known-good, integrity-checked version, and it stands out because the harness pins its Go dependencies and its remote skills/agents by commit SHA. The generated `.mcp.json` carries the same unpinned entry.
policy_rule_text: Package registry (npm/`npx`, Go module proxy, Homebrew) | Yes | No | Fetching yaah itself and third-party MCP server packages; MUST resolve pinned, integrity-checked versions.
Third-party MCP servers MUST be pinned to a known-good, integrity-checked version — no server package auto-installed afresh, unpinned, on every run.

evidence.snippet: Args `["-y", "@context7/mcp"]` — no @version pin; npx -y installs latest on each launch
context7 server emitted as command npx args ["-y","@context7/mcp"], unpinned in generated project config

recommended_actions: Pin the `context7` package to a specific version (e.g. `@context7/mcp@<version>`) in `pkg/mcp/providers/context7.go` and prefer an integrity-checked install, matching the pinning applied to Go deps and remote skills.

findings.summary: The optional Notion MCP provider inlines a literal bearer token into the generated MCP config rather than referencing it indirectly.
findings.description: `NewNotion` embeds the operator-supplied API token directly into the `OPENAPI_MCP_HEADERS` env value as `"Authorization": "Bearer <token>"`, which the generators serialize verbatim into the version-controllable MCP config files (`.mcp.json` / settings) — a secret-in-file path that violates the vault-not-files principle. The provider is off by default (it requires a token) and Notion is not one of the remit's default authorized counterparties, so this is a latent design gap rather than a live exposure, but the code path writes a credential into a committed-config surface whenever enabled.

tags.label: Secrets stored in vault, not in files

evidence.snippet: Env OPENAPI_MCP_HEADERS built as `{"Authorization": "Bearer ` + n.apiToken + `", ...}` — literal token embedded in config-bound env value

recommended_actions: Reference the Notion token via an env-var indirection the runtime resolves at launch (e.g. `${NOTION_TOKEN}`) instead of inlining its value, and document that enabling Notion adds a counterparty outside the remit's default allowlist so the operator opts in explicitly.

findings.summary: The one write-capable built-in MCP tool, `yaah_planning_init`, writes to the workspace with no harness-enforced approval gate.
findings.description: `addPlanningInitTool` creates the `.planning/` directory tree and writes `STATE.md` immediately on invocation, with no confirmation step inside yaah; the remit lists `yaah_planning_init` as a restricted tool whose workspace writes require the developer's review. Protection depends entirely on the host runtime's MCP permission prompt rather than anything yaah enforces. Impact is low — the tool only scaffolds planning directories and a state file — but the harness-side approval the remit expects is absent.
policy_rule_text: High-impact actions — destructive shell commands, file writes, and MCP tools that write, send, or execute — MUST reach a human checkpoint before they run.

evidence.snippet: addPlanningInitTool (lines 363-413) mkdir's .planning subtree and WriteFile's STATE.md directly, no approval/confirmation branch

recommended_actions: Gate `yaah_planning_init` behind an explicit confirmation or dry-run-plus-approve flow within the tool handler, or document that it relies on the host MCP permission prompt so operators do not allowlist it blindly.

findings.summary: The session record's `Findings[]` field is never populated, so non-blocking security findings are absent from the durable audit log.
findings.description: `session.Session` declares a `Findings []Finding` array for detected secrets, lint issues, and comment findings, but `HandleHookEvent` only appends to `ToolCalls`, `BlockedCalls`, and `FilesModified` — it never writes `Findings`. A finding is captured in the durable log only when it also blocks (recorded as `BlockedCalls[].Reason`); a non-blocking lint or comment finding is surfaced to stderr and lost from the record. This narrows the remit's Log-Only requirement that security findings be recorded to the per-session audit log, though the core tool-call/block/file-mod record remains intact.
policy_rule_text: Every hook event — tool calls, blocked commands, file modifications, and security findings — is recorded to the durable per-session audit log, whether or not it also raised an alert.

evidence.snippet: Session struct declares `Findings []Finding` (line 14) with a Finding type carrying Type/File/Line/Message/Severity
session update block (lines 129-156) appends ToolCalls/BlockedCalls/FilesModified but never sess.Findings

recommended_actions: In `HandleHookEvent`, parse each handler `Result` into `session.Finding` entries and append them to `sess.Findings` before saving, so non-blocking secret/lint/comment findings are recorded in the durable audit log.

findings.summary: No test asserts that every generated target carries the full protective hook set — the exact untested property behind the Codex omission.
findings.description: The generator package has per-target unit tests (`codex_test.go`, `opencode_test.go`, `copilot_test.go`) and the hook registry is tested, but the protective handlers themselves (`guard.go`, `secretscan.go`) carry no tests and no test verifies that each agent's generated hook config actually contains the command guard and secret scanner. The remit's expected pattern calls for adversarial testing that confirms a generated configuration genuinely carries the protections it claims; the Codex hook gap is direct evidence that this parity property was never exercised.
policy_rule_text: The project publishes a threat model and a security-disclosure process, and its own protections are adversarially tested — confirming the secret scanner catches a planted secret, the command guard blocks a catastrophic command, a poisoning write into a session-loaded file is prevented, and a generated configuration genuinely carries the protections it claims.

evidence.snippet: Codex generator test exists but does not assert the emitted hooks.json carries PreToolUse/PostToolUse (the command guard + secret scanner); the gap shipped untested
handlers directory ships guard.go and secretscan.go with no corresponding *_test.go — the blocking controls have no adversarial/regression tests

recommended_actions: Add a cross-target test that generates each agent's config and asserts the command guard and secret scanner appear in the emitted hooks for every target, plus behavioral tests that the guard blocks a planted catastrophic command and the scanner blocks a planted secret.

positives.title: Command guard blocks catastrophic shell commands at the tool-call boundary
positives.description: `NewCommandGuard` registers seven catastrophic-command patterns and the PreToolUse handler returns a blocking result (`Block: true`) before a matched Bash command executes — a real code-level gate, active on Claude, OpenCode, and Copilot output.

positives.title: Secret scanner blocks credential-bearing edits and never reprints the value
positives.description: The PostToolUse secret scanner detects a dozen credential patterns in edited files and returns a blocking result; it reports only file, line, and pattern label, never the secret value itself.

positives.title: Durable structured per-session audit log
positives.description: `session.Store` persists each session as `.claude/sessions/<id>.json` with timestamped tool calls, blocked calls, and files-modified via atomic temp-write-and-rename, and IDs are sanitized against path traversal — a reconstructable, action-level audit trail.

positives.title: Pinned, integrity-checked dependency baseline
positives.description: Go dependencies are pinned in `go.mod` with a committed `go.sum` carrying module hashes and a deliberately small tree; remote skills and agents are pinned by full commit SHA rather than mutable refs.

positives.title: Remote MCP over TLS
positives.description: The wired-in remote `pulumi` MCP server is configured with an `https://` endpoint, satisfying the remit's requirement that remote MCP connections use TLS.

positives.title: Clean built-in MCP tool descriptions (no tool poisoning)
positives.description: The seven `yaah` MCP tool descriptions state only what each tool does, with no instruction-like language directed at the model — the built-in server is not a tool-poisoning surface.

rows.purpose: durable per-session audit — timestamped tool calls, blocked commands, files modified, event count
session lifecycle events (SessionStart / SessionEnd / Stop) with timestamp, event name, session ID, cwd

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Partial. yaah has real, operative protections — a command guard and secret scanner that block at the tool-call boundary in Go code, a durable structured session audit log, pinned Go dependencies and commit-pinned remote skills, and TLS-only remote MCP — which lift Supply Chain, Monitor, and the well-bounded Domain to Established. Implement Zero Trust is held down to Partial because one of four generated targets (Codex) ships with the enforcing hooks entirely absent, the command guard's coverage is a narrow bypassable denylist, and the one write-capable built-in MCP tool has no harness-level approval gate. Build an AI Red Team is the weakest category: the protective handlers carry no adversarial or protection-parity tests, and the Codex omission is direct evidence that the "protections carry across every target" property was never tested.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: yaah is a tightly-scoped local CLI that does no inference; its tool inventory is a bounded set of check-only MCP tools plus five hook handlers, enforced structurally in typed Go rather than a prompt, and its non-goals (no networked service, no exfiltration) are explicit. Minor scope creep — the optional `notion` provider is not in the remit's default allowlist — keeps it from Strong.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: yaah ingests no external content into an LLM context (it runs no model) and its data sources — the local repo, pinned git remotes for remote skills/agents, and opted-in MCP endpoints — are controlled; the secret scanner guards edited files. No vector or embedding store exists, so LLM08 does not apply.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: The command guard (PreToolUse, blocking) and secret scanner (PostToolUse, blocking) are genuine code-level gates that run on the agent's path, but the Codex generator ships neither (`hookmap.go` blank Pre/PostToolUse), the guard's denylist is seven bypassable regex patterns, and `yaah_planning_init` writes to the workspace with no harness-enforced approval — so enforcement is real but non-uniform and incomplete.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: Go dependencies are pinned with a committed `go.sum` (integrity hashes) and a small tree, remote skills/agents are pinned by full commit SHA, and the remote `pulumi` MCP is reached over TLS; the specific accepted gap is the `context7` MCP server fetched unpinned via `npx -y @context7/mcp` on every run.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: The generator and registry packages carry unit tests, but the protective handlers (`guard.go`, `secretscan.go`) have none and no test asserts that each generated target carries the full protective hook set — the untested property whose absence is exactly the Codex hook omission; there is no evidence of adversarial testing driving design.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: `pkg/session` persists a durable, structured, timestamped per-session record (`.claude/sessions/<id>.json`) of tool calls, blocked calls, and file modifications via atomic writes — reconstructable and action-level; the specific gap is that the record's `Findings[]` field is never populated, so non-blocking security findings are surfaced only to stderr.
